coredata.dc:title: Training Gaussian process regression through data augmentation for battery SOC estimation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Santoni, Francesco
De Angelis, Alessio
Moschitta, Antonio
Carbone, Paolo
coredata.dc:description: Estimating the state of charge of a battery is generally a challenging operation, as it depends non-linearly on the parameters that describe the internal state of the battery under test. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy provides useful data for estimating state of charge, however, there is no simple relation between impedance spectra and state of charge. This relationship can be determined through the use of Machine Learning techniques. In particular, Gaussian process regression is used in this work. For the method to be efficient, the training ensemble must be quite large, but spectroscopy measurements can be time-consuming. A Monte Carlo ensemble generated from a limited set of experimental data was then used to train the model. Two sets of input features were used for the Gaussian process: components of the impedance spectrum, and equivalent circuit parameters. Both features give the same accuracy in estimating the state of charge, even of batteries whose impedance spectra are not included in the training set. The first set of features has the advantage of not requiring model fitting. The second feature set has a significantly shorter training time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion batteries
State of charge
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Machine learning
Gaussian process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impedance data and definition of input features
Generating Monte Carlo spectra and parameters

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the European Union\u2013Next Generation EU under Grant PRIN 2022KB2HZM established by the Italian Ministry of University and Research (MUR).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Block diagram of the applied method.
Left: scheme of the custom EIS board. Right: a picture of the implemented board with heatsink and fan support mounted on it.
A sample of the data extracted from the dataset in [19].
The battery impedance equivalent circuit model used in this work.
Markers: some experimental impedance points of a single battery. Lines: model (1) fit.
Impedance curves of all batteries at SOC 100% measured six times each.

Correlations between real part of impedance values. Top: correlations between frequency components at SOC 100% and 50%. Bottom: correlations of frequency components 0.1 and 10 Hz for all SOCs. The colors represent the values of Pearson’s coefficient R . The diameter of the circles represents the value of
| R |
(the diameter of the circles on the diagonal corresponds to
| R |
= 1
).

Correlations between EC parameters. Top: correlations between EC parameters at SOC 100% and 50%. Bottom: correlations of
R
1
and
Q
1
for all SOCs. The colors represent the values of Pearson’s coefficient R . The diameter of the circles represents the value of
| R |
(the diameter of the circles on the diagonal corresponds to
| R |
= 1
).
Monte Carlo spectra of a single battery at SOCs 100%, 90% and 80%.
Cumulative distribution function of the error affecting SOC estimation. The SOC for 150 samples was estimated using the model trained for different cardinalities of the training ensemble.
Leave-one-out cross-validation: cumulative distribution function of the error on SOC estimation. The overall error of all iterations is reported.
Predicted SOC for thirty observations. Error bars represent 95% confidence level obtained from the GPR.
RMS error on SOC estimation for different cardinality of the training ensemble.
Leave-one-out cross-validation: RMS error on SOC estimation. Iterations are denoted as XXX_Y (XXX: training batteries. Y: test battery).
Leave-one-out cross-validation for SOC 20%–90%: RMS error on SOC estimation. Iterations are denoted as XXX_Y (XXX: training batteries. Y: test battery).
Leave-one-out cross-validation for SOC 20%–80%: RMS error on SOC estimation. Iterations are denoted as XXX_Y (XXX: training batteries. Y: test battery).

head.ce:title: Training Gaussian process regression through data augmentation for battery SOC estimation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia, Perugia, 06125, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering, University of Perugia, Perugia, 06125, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Estimating the state of charge of a battery is generally a challenging operation, as it depends non-linearly on the parameters that describe the internal state of the battery under test. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy provides useful data for estimating state of charge, however, there is no simple relation between impedance spectra and state of charge. This relationship can be determined through the use of Machine Learning techniques. In particular, Gaussian process regression is used in this work. For the method to be efficient, the training ensemble must be quite large, but spectroscopy measurements can be time-consuming. A Monte Carlo ensemble generated from a limited set of experimental data was then used to train the model. Two sets of input features were used for the Gaussian process: components of the impedance spectrum, and equivalent circuit parameters. Both features give the same accuracy in estimating the state of charge, even of batteries whose impedance spectra are not included in the training set. The first set of features has the advantage of not requiring model fitting. The second feature set has a significantly shorter training time.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing use of batteries in automotive and grid energy storage systems means that increasingly versatile and effective battery management systems need to be developed concurrently. In particular, diagnostic systems capable of estimating the state of charge (SOC), state of health (SOH), and remaining useful life (RUL) of batteries are demanded.

Machine learning techniques have been variously applied to the problem of estimating the state of charge and health of batteries [1,2]. Various features can be extracted from the measured current and voltage signals across the battery as inputs to regression or classification models. Features of interest can be obtained from both time-domain and frequency-domain data. In [3], more than thirty features are extracted from current and voltage data measured during charge and discharge processes, using data from four public datasets. The features are used to estimate SOH by four different machine learning methods: random forest (RF), deep neural network ensemble (dNNe), Bayesian ridge regression (BRR) and Gaussian process regression (GPR). In [4], batteries are cycled under the worldwide light duty driving test cycle (WLTC) load profile in order to acquire real-world driving data. Condition indicators are extracted from both time-domain and frequency domain data. Condition indicators are fed as inputs to the GPR method to predict the SOH. Internal features of a battery can be extracted from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data [5,6]. In [7], the capacity extracted from EIS spectra is fed to the GPR to predict the RUL. In [8], equivalent circuit parameters of a battery are extracted from EIS spectra and used to estimate the SOC through GPR and support vector machine (SVM). In [9], the real and imaginary part of EIS spectra for a set of frequencies are directly fed to the GPR to estimate the SOC. Analogous data are used in [10,11] to predict the SOC through a deep learning architecture called Deep Residual Network. In [12], the battery is modeled as an equivalent circuit consisting of two RC loops in series. Circuit parameters are obtained from EIS analysis. Battery discharge model and current measurements are used with a Kalman filter to estimate the SOC. A similar approach is used in [13].

This work focuses on EIS data. One difficulty in using machine learning with this type of data is that the time required for measurements, and thus for preparing training ensembles, is quite long. In addition to this, EIS spectra can show some variability even between batteries of the same model, and therefore training data should be measured on more than one battery so as to cover the entire range of variation. In [9,14], the GPR method is used to predict the SOC of different lithium-ion batteries with good accuracy, but training was repeated separately for each battery. Instead, it would be important to demonstrate that it can accurately predict the SOC of one battery using a model trained on data from other batteries. The purpose of this paper is to demonstrate the possibility of obtaining extended training ensembles by generating Monte Carlo data from a restricted set of experimental data, and to show the ability of the GPR model to accurately predict the SOC of a battery not used in the training ensemble.
The method is defined as shown by the block diagram in Fig. 1:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Two set of features have been used as input data of the regression model: the real and imaginary part of the impedance spectrum (feature 1), equivalent circuit (EC) model parameters (feature 2). For feature 2, an EC model commonly used in literature has been selected. Model parameters are estimated by fitting experimental spectra through a non-linear least square method with multiple starting values.

In order to perform data augmentation, the mean and covariance of experimental spectra are computed from the data. The covariance matrix accounts for fluctuations among repeated measurements, and correlations among different SOCs. Experimental mean and covariance are used to define a multivariate Gaussian distribution (MGD). A Monte Carlo statistics of impedance spectra is generated by randomly extracting values from the experimental MGD; 200 random spectra are generated for each battery. An analogous method is used to generate a Monte Carlo statistics of EC parameters.

The Gaussian process regression model has been trained and tested with both feature 1 and feature 2. The model has been trained with different sets in order to find the minimum set cardinality needed to get accurate results. A cross-validation method has been applied. From the data of four batteries, the model has been trained with the data of three batteries, leaving one battery out as a test, repeating the procedure for each battery.

ce:section.ce:para: It will be shown that regression allows predictions of equal accuracy for the two groups of features. This aspect may be useful in diagnostic systems where EIS is not performed, but circuit parameters are extracted from signals in the time domain [15,16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impedance data and definition of input features

ce:section.ce:para:
EIS measurements have been performed on a set of Samsung ICR18650-26J lithium ion batteries. The measuring system is based on the custom EIS board shown in Fig. 2, that was already used in previous works [17,18]. It is a compact board based on a Howland current pump, i.e. a current generator driven by means of a control voltage
V
c t r l
; the generated current is
I = g
V
c t r l
, where g is the transconductance gain.
V
c t r l
is supplied by an external waveform generator. A multisine signal is used to drive the current pump, stimulating simultaneously 14 frequency components f = [0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 1, 2, 4, 10, 20, 40, 100, 200, 400, 1000]
T
Hz
. The current I is injected into the battery through the shunt resistor
R
S
. The voltages across
R
S
and the battery are amplified by two instrumentation amplifiers (INA). An external DAQ board is used to acquire both voltages. The impedance spectrum is obtained as the ratio of the Fourier transforms of the voltage across the battery and the injected current.

As discussed in Section 2.2, parameters of EC models are estimated by fitting 100 repeated measurements of the impedance of a single battery. We used the same 100 impedance spectra already analyzed in [17]. For calibration and test of Gaussian process regression we instead used a dataset that we already made publicly available, collected on a set of 4 batteries [19]. During the measurements, the batteries were kept in a temperature-controlled chamber at
25 ± 1
° C
. For each battery, the impedance has been measured for 10 different SOCs, from 10% to 100% of the capacity, at 10% spaced intervals. For each battery, 6 repeated measurements have been performed for all SOCs. A sample of the data from [19] is shown in Fig. 3. The real and imaginary part of these spectra at all observed frequencies (2 × 14 values) are used as input (feature 1) to the Gaussian process regression for SOC estimation.

It is well known that EC models do not reflect all the components of the internal structure of a battery. Nonetheless, they are effective models accounting for observed voltage, current and impedance [20,21]. We selected a model commonly used in literature [21]. EC model selection and fitting was discussed in detail in [22]. As shown on the Nyquist plots in Fig. 3, impedance curves have a single arc, hence we supposed that a circuit with a single Randles element (Fig. 4) is sufficient to model the battery. The impedance model is given by:
(1)
Z
s
=
R
0
+ s L +
R
1
Q
1
s
α
1
+ 1
Q
1
s
α
1
+
R
1
Q
1
Q
2
s
α
1
+
α
2
+
Q
2
s
α
2
,
where
s = i ω
( ω being the angular frequency), the impedance of constant phase elements (CPE) is
Z
C P E
=
1
Q
s
α
. When
α = 1
, the CPE is an ideal capacitor whose capacitance is Q . CPE
1
describes ion transport across the electrolyte, producing the low frequency straight line on the rightmost part of the plot in Fig. 3, while CPE
2
is a model of the double layer formed at the electrode/electrolyte interface.
R
0
is the contact resistance;
R
1
is the charge-transfer resistance on the electrode surface; L is an inductance. Hence, the model is defined by 7 parameters.

Curve fitting is performed by minimizing the residual:
(2)
‖ r ‖
2
=
∑
s
Z
̃
′
s
−
Z
′
s , Θ
2
+
Z
̃
′ ′
s
−
Z
′ ′
s , Θ
2
,
where
Z
′
= ℜ e
Z
,
Z
′ ′
= ℑ m
Z
,
Z
̃
is the experimental impedance described in Section 2.1, Θ is the vector of parameters to be estimated by minimizing the residual, and the summation is extended over all observed frequencies s . Therefore the estimator is:
(3)
Θ
ˆ
=
arg min
Θ
‖ r ‖
2
.

This is a non-linear least square problem that we solved with Matlab by using the function lsqnonlin, which implements a trust-region reflective algorithm; the non-linear problem was initialized by means of the method MultiStart, performing a randomized grid search to find best initial conditions.

As proved in [22], model (1) is always identifiable if the frequency range is sufficiently wide, such as the one we selected. In order to evaluate the goodness-of-fit for each curve, the root-mean-square-error (RMSE) has been computed, i.e.
RMSE =
‖ r ‖
2
/
N
f
, where
N
f
= 28
is the number of frequency components on the real and imaginary axes. On average, an RMSE value of 3
× 1
0
− 4
was obtained, appropriate to accept model (1). Fitting results for some example impedance points are reported in Fig. 5.

Fig. 6 shows the impedance curves of the four batteries, each measured six times, at SOC 100%. Although the batteries are all the same model, the curves are not all overlapping, with systematic differences beyond random fluctuations. This variability precludes using data from only one battery to train a regression model with which to predict the SOC of all other batteries. Instead, it is necessary to train the regression model on a set of batteries large enough to cover the variability affecting the battery model. It is crucial that the training set includes this variability domain as extensively as possible, in order to be capable of identifying the SOC of different batteries. Indeed, in a preliminary stage, we used a lesser number of batteries, obtaining less accurate results. We increased the number of batteries and of repeated measurements, until we obtained acceptably accurate results. In Section 4 it will be shown how by training the model with three batteries it is possible to predict the SOC of the remaining battery, whose data do not enter the training set at all. This conclusion will be confirmed by repeating the same procedure four times cyclically, each time using a different battery as test (leave-one-out cross-validation).

Also in Section 4, it will be shown that it is by no means sufficient for accurate results to train the regression model using only six impedance curves for each battery. The cardinality of the training set must therefore be increased. Since measuring the impedance spectrum for all SOCs takes several hours, the only practical way to significantly extend the cardinality is to generate a Monte Carlo statistics, as described in the following.

The statistical distribution of the experimental data must be determined in order to generate the Monte Carlo dataset with the same distribution. The distribution was determined under the following assumptions:

ce:list-item.ce:para: According to the analysis in [17], the noise affecting both real and imaginary part of the impedance spectrum is Gaussian.
According to the analysis in [22], the noise affecting EC model parameters can be assumed to be Gaussian.
As shown in the following, impedance values at different SOCs and frequencies are correlated, as well as are parameter values at different SOCs

ce:section.ce:para:
In accordance with these assumptions, a multivariate Gaussian distribution is assumed to describe experimental data. A different MGD is defined for each battery. Mean and covariance of each distribution are estimated from six repeated measurements. Just to give some examples, correlations between frequency components of impedance spectra and between EC parameters are shown in Figs. 7 and 8 by means of the Pearson correlation coefficient R . The examples clearly show that the covariance between different components or parameters is not in general zero, that therefore Monte Carlo statistics cannot be generated for each variable independently, and that MGD must be used. Let us indicate SOC values
100 % , 90 % , … , 10 %
with
c = 1 , 2 , … , 10
, and define observation vectors as multidimensional random variables:
(4)
Z

.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generating Monte Carlo spectra and parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Mean and covariance (5) were calculated separately for each of the four batteries in the dataset [19]. Random spectra and parameters were extracted from the distribution (6) using the Matlab function mvnrnd. Sample generation is a fast process. On a common laptop it takes time in the order of minutes to generate hundreds of samples. As an example, Monte Carlo spectra of a single battery at SOC 100%, 90% and 80% are reported in Fig. 9; for each SOC, 20 samples are reported. It is evident that the distributions corresponding to distinct SOC are overlapping, and this leads to an error in the estimation of SOC through Gaussian process regression, as it will be shown below. This error in estimation is unavoidable, being related to the natural variability of batteries.

Gaussian process regression is a well-known interpolation method used in machine learning. Its main aspects and results are recalled and presented here with reference to the standard textbook on the subject [23]. A Gaussian process is defined as any collection of random variables, each subset of which has a MGD. A set of Gaussian distributed random functions
f
x
is defined by:
(7)
m
x
= E
f
x
,
k
x ,
x
′
= E
f
x
− m
x
f
x
′
− m
x
′
,
where
m
x
is the mean function, and the covariance function
k
x ,
x
′
is called the kernel function. The probability density function of
f
x
is thus
p
f
≡ φ
f
x
m
x
, k
x ,
x
′
. A set of observations of the independent variable x is described by the matrix
X ∈
R
k × n
, where k is the number of components, and n the number of observations. For the present work, each column of X is a sample of
Z
̃
c
or
Θ
ˆ
c
as defined in (4) and extracted from the Monte Carlo set illustrated in the previous section. Let us also define
y = f
X
+ ɛ
a vector of observed values of the unknown function f evaluated on the observed X with an additive Gaussian noise distributed as
φ
ɛ
0 ,
σ
n
2
I
, where I is the
n × n
identity matrix. In this work, the unknown function is the relation between features
Z
̃
c
or
Θ
ˆ
c
from the Monte Carlo dataset and the SOC of batteries; y values are the values of the SOC. Gaussian process regression is the problem of finding the a posteriori probability density of the function given the observations:
p
f
y , X
. The mode of
p
f
y , X
is the best estimate of the unknown function. Let us call
y
∗
a vector of values of the unknown function that have to be predicted for a set of new observations
X
∗
. Assuming that all the data are standardized, we can thus write the a priori probability density:
(8)

. In Gaussian process regression, training the model means calculating the a posteriori probability density given the observations X . It can be proved that the posterior is:

Finally, all that remains is to choose the appropriate kernel. Intuitively, the meaning of the kernel is as follows: when two inputs
x
i
and
x
j
are similar, the outputs
f
x
i
and
f
x
j
are also expected to be similar. Typically, one selects a kernel that is a function only of the distance
|
x
i m
−
x
j m
|
( m being the component index of
x
i
); in this case, the kernel is also called radial basis function. Although the most used kernel is the squared exponential
(11)

;
σ
f
and
σ
m
are hyperparameters also called length scales. The vertical scale
σ
f
is a measure of the dispersion of the values of the function; the horizontal scales
σ
m
are a measure of the correlation between two input points
x
i
and
x
j
. Hyperparameters must be optimized on observations during model training. This is done by maximizing the following logarithmic likelihood:
(13)
log p

log 2 π .

Gaussian process regression is implemented in the Statistics and Machine Learning Toolbox of Matlab. The command fitrgp is used to train the model on a set of observations and to optimize the hyperparameters. Then, the command predict is used to predict the SOC for a set of new observations by means of the trained model. The model has been trained on a workstation mounting 16 cores/32 threads CPU with a clock frequency of 2.8 GHz. Optimization has been performed by taking advantage of the parallel computing capabilities available in the Parallel Computing Toolbox of Matlab. The number of threads allocated is selected automatically by the software. In this case, Matlab allocated 12 threads.

The suitable cardinality of the training ensemble is a trade-off between the accuracy of the model and the computation time required to train it. If the model is trained on a few samples, it generally does not allow accurate predictions on new samples drawn from the same distribution. Therefore, a preliminary analysis was carried out by training the model several times with a different number of samples. Three batteries were used, for each of them the MGD was obtained, and a Monte Carlo ensemble was generated as described in Section 3. From the union of the three ensembles,
N
t r
= 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 training samples were randomly drawn, and further different
N
t e s t
= 150 samples for validation. The whole procedure has been performed for both features 1 and 2.

The estimation error is defined as the absolute value of the difference between estimated and true SOC. The empirical cumulative distribution function of the error is reported in Fig. 10, while the RMSE is reported in Table 1. The observed RMSE is the same as that obtained in [9], where the GPR was fed with feature 1. It is noted that training the model with 200 samples instead of 150 produces results that are only marginally better. Considering that, for feature 1, training the model with 150 samples takes 4–5 h, while with 200 samples it takes more than 12 h, we chose to always use 150 training samples for the cross-validation with four batteries described below. Training takes hours, but the computation time for prediction is
< 0 . 1
s, suitable for real-time monitoring.

The leave-one-out cross-validation has been performed for both feature 1 and 2: a battery is selected as test, the other three are used for training; the process is repeated iteratively for all batteries. The Monte Carlo dataset is used for training, while the test is executed directly on experimental data. The error affecting SOC estimation is computed as in the previous section. In Table 2, the RMS error for each iteration and the overall error is reported. In Fig. 11, the cumulative distribution function of the overall error of all iterations is reported. As an exampe, in Fig. 12, predicted SOC for thirty observations is reported. As can be seen from the Table 2 and Fig. 11, the accuracy of the regression is essentially the same for both features. Feature 1 has the advantage of not requiring assumptions on the equivalent circuit model, while the regression based on feature 2, being based on fewer inputs, can be trained much faster (about 20 min with 150 samples). Contrary to the results of Section 4.2, the RMS error is greater than that obtained in [9]. This is explained by the fact that we tested the GPR on batteries not included in the training ensemble, i.e. a realistic use case not considered in [9].

We repeated the analysis by restricting the SOC within ranges 20%–90% and 20%–80%. Results are shown in Tables 3 and 4. A slight error reduction can be noticed, probably motivated by the exclusion of the states of charge at which the battery voltage is more unstable. In any case, the observed error reduction is negligible in practical applications, and the full SOC range can always be used.

Finally, as a reference in order to reimplement the model for analogous applications, the model parameters for both Feature 1 and 2 are reported in Tables 5 and 6. The noise variance
σ
n
2
, the vertical scale
σ
f
2
and the length scales
σ
m
2
of kernel (12) were fitted by maximizing the likelihood (13).

We used the measured EIS spectra of four batteries to estimate their SOC by feeding a regression model based on Gaussian process with two different sets of features. We have proved that from a limited set of experimental data it is possible to generate a Monte Carlo ensemble that makes it possible to train the regression model. The natural variability in the properties of batteries, albeit of the same model, makes it necessary to have, as a starting experimental basis, a set of batteries that is sufficiently varied, so that the corresponding Monte Carlo ensemble is adequate to train a regression model that makes accurate predictions on batteries of the same model, but not used in the training set.
An important extension of this work is to apply the presented method to the critical task of estimating the SOH.

Francesco Santoni: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Alessio De Angelis: Writing – review & editing, Software, Data curation, Conceptualization. Antonio Moschitta: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Paolo Carbone: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Paolo Carbone reports financial support was provided by European Union. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the European Union–Next Generation EU under Grant PRIN 2022KB2HZM established by the Italian Ministry of University and Research (MUR).
